fact: January 5 is exactly 5 days into the new year, which means you've already lived through 0.68% of the entire year!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is when many squirrels are typically getting extra chunky because they're burning through their fat reserves to stay warm during the coldest part of winter.
The sun is still rising later and setting earlier than it will in just a few weeks - we're still losing precious daylight even though the winter solstice already passed!
January 5 falls in the middle of Capricorn season, making it a day ruled by the sea-goat - a mythical creature that's half goat, half fish!
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, January 5 is peak summer, which means kids are probably at the beach while kids up north are building snowmen.
This date marks when many evergreen trees in cold climates typically have their needles coated in a special waxy layer that's extra thick - nature's version of winter lip balm!
Polar bears in the Arctic are usually at their fluffiest around this time because their fur has reached maximum winter thickness - they're basically walking fur balls!
January 5 is roughly when Earth reaches its closest point to the sun each year, called perihelion - so we're actually getting more solar energy even though it feels freezing cold up north!
Many hibernating animals like groundhogs are typically in their deepest sleep phase right now, with their heart rates slowed down to just 5 beats per minute!
In northern regions, January 5 is often when ice on ponds and lakes reaches its thickest point of the winter - perfect for ice fishing or skating!
